data.title: Finger-lickin’ good KFC on the Christmas menu for millions in Japan
data.description: Christmas season in Japan often means a KFC feast for millions of Japanese families celebrating the holiday.\nColonel Sanders’ restaurant has been part of the Christmas tradition since the 1970s in Japan, where the holiday is mainly a secular festival for consumerism.\n

data.title: The brutal life of child recruits under a shadowy rebel group in DR Congo
data.description:
Forcibly recruited into a rebel militia affiliated with Islamic State, two boys have revealed the “torment” of living in its camps as members committed massacres in the Democratic Republic of Congo’s northeast.
The two minors freed from the Allied Democratic Forces (ADF) gave Agence France-Presse an unprecedented account of the shadowy group, notorious for its extreme brutality.
Paluku, a frail 12-year-old, spent two months with the ADF after rebels killed his mother during an attack on his village in eastern North Kivu province. His brother and sister were also captured.
Edouard, 17, spent a gruelling four years with the ADF - formed by Ugandan rebels who took refuge in DRC - after he was kidnapped at age 12.

The two boys, using pseudonyms, spoke on condition of anonymity at a centre specialising in the care of minors recruited by armed groups in the region, whose location Agence France-Presse has chosen not to disclose to avoid potential reprisals.
Their accounts were confirmed by health and security sources.
Round-faced Edouard, a fast-talker, did not mince his words in describing his years of “torment” within the ADF.
“We suffered terribly,” he said.
After their capture, Edouard and Paluku were sent to ADF bases hidden in the dense forest of northeast DRC where the elusive rebels avoid patrols by the Congolese army and Ugandan forces deployed there since 2021.
The bases consist of simple tents and tarps, easy to move in the event of an attack.

Most occupants are women and children, according to security sources, contributing to the group’s operations - but also serving as human shields.
New recruits are swiftly forced to convert to Islam and learn Arabic, but also English and Swahili, Edouard said.
“I was also trained in medicine to treat the wounded, and we learned how to handle weapons and clean them,” he said.
Paluku said he underwent similar training, as well as learning how to “steal food, clothing and medicine to bring back to the ADF camp”.
Children play a central role in supplying the group, security sources said. Those who fail to bring back loot face severe punishment.
The wives of the ADF commanders, some of whom are particularly influential, also exercise power over the young recruits.

When the fighters go out on “operations”, the youngest among them like Paluku, were “supposed to bring something back for the chief’s wife,” he said, like soap, cooking oil or fabric.
“To get it we have to loot people’s belongings, and if a chief’s wife accuses you to her husband of not bringing back what she asked for, she can demand that you be killed,” he said.
Edouard and Paluku said they were subjected to incessant corporal punishment.
Girls and boys were whipped or thrown into pits for several weeks over the slightest misbehaviour.
“I was punished with lashes because I refused to go kill people,” Paluku said with a long stare.
Edouard took part in combat with the group at least three times against the Congolese army or local militias.


“They beat us mostly when we lost our weapons and ammunition, claiming we had wasted them for nothing or lost them on the front,” he explained.
Faced with such an accusation, Edouard said a chief ordered that he be whipped.
“I fell ill because of those lashes. I told the chief outright I was no longer able to go fight on the front, I begged him to send others who were capable, but that made him even more angry, and I was whipped once again,” he said.
About 10 children freed from the ADF arrive on average each month at the reception centre in the troubled northeast Ituri province.
“These children have suffered psychological trauma and torture, and when they arrive here, most are aggressive,” said Madeleine, a psychologist at the centre.
After a few weeks spent around other children and staff, their aggression fades, she said.
But there are other scars to contend with.
Edouard became addicted to drugs administered by the rebels after he was wounded in combat.
Suffering from speech disorders, he talks constantly and sometimes incoherently, disturbing other residents, Madeleine said.
After a year at the centre receiving ongoing treatment, Edouard recounted the horrors of his experience with a shy smile and a lively, excitable gaze.
had a darker expression, recalling his sister who remains a hostage.
“She has become the wife of one of the ADF chiefs,” he said.

data.title: Hong Kong restaurant sales drop 10% over Christmas as cross-border trips hit 1.2 million
data.description:
Hong Kong’s food and beverage sector recorded a 10 per cent year on year drop in business over the Christmas period, an industry leader said, as 1.2 million trips were made through the city’s borders on the first day of the long holiday.
But the city’s hospitality sector appeared to fare better, with one hotel group saying two of its properties recorded more than 90 per cent occupancy and a slight 5 per cent increase in room tariffs.
Simon Wong Ka-wo, president of the Hong Kong Federation of Restaurants and Related Trades, told a radio show on Friday that the city’s dining establishments struggled to attract customers because many locals had travelled across the border for the holidays.
“My estimate would be that there was about a 10 per cent decrease over the Christmas period, but if we look at just December 24 and 25, it’s more than 10 per cent,” Wong said.
“The main reason, I believe, was that residents have started leaving the city since last Friday. With them leaving in droves, of course that’d affect the industry. Another reason is the lowered spending desire and power.”
He pointed out that it was not just that of Hong Kong residents spending less, but also visitors from mainland China.
He observed that the spending of tourists in Hong Kong during Christmas was lower by 20 per cent compared with before the pandemic.
“While there are more visitors from the mainland, which is of course a good thing, and could replenish some of our outflow, the weakening of spending power is a big blow to us,” Wong said.
He said that restaurants in areas such as Tsim Sha Tsui, Central, Sheung Wan, Wan Chai and Causeway Bay might have more business because of the presence of Christmas celebration activities, but the ones in other areas were very quiet.
Wong was also not confident about business over New Year’s Eve because the countdown fireworks were cancelled, and many would have taken leave to make a longer vacation with Christmas holidays and travel.

Alan Chan Chung-yee, chief operations officer of Miramar Group and a lawmaker-elect, described the northbound spending trend as a “serious” issue, given how Hongkongers were flocking to the mainland for cheaper food and services.
But Chan said that the group’s hotel in Tsim Sha Tsui had an occupancy rate of 90 per cent with the remaining rooms under renovation, so it was “essentially full” even with a 5 per cent tariff rise, while their branch in Causeway Bay was 95 per cent full with a 10 per cent tariff increase.
He attributed the rise in room occupancy to the delayed business travel to the city that resulted from trade uncertainties earlier in the year, with the array of US tariffs for different countries, and the increase in mainland tourists due to the Beijing-Tokyo spat over Taiwan.
Relationships between China and Japan had gone sour after Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said last month that a conflict in the Taiwan Strait might justify military intervention by her country.
Chan said over 40 per cent of his customers were from the mainland, with some of them – especially those under the age of 40 – drawn by Christmas celebrations in Hong Kong, which was perhaps the most festive place across China.
Chan pointed out that if the 100 daily quota for the southbound vehicle scheme from Guangdong into Hong Kong’s urban area, which opened three days ago, was running smoothly, the government should consider raising the quota soon.

data.title: Israel’s rising air defence sales become its ‘most potent’ foreign policy weapon
data.description:
Israel is cashing in on soaring international demand for combat-proven air defence systems, as analysts say the billions of dollars' worth of weapon sales will significantly expand the country’s influence beyond the Middle East.
The weapon exports to Europe, Asia and Africa are helping Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s administration overcome its diplomatic isolation over the Gaza war by making the Jewish-majority state a key long-term security partner for its customers.
“Exporting air defence systems is the highest level of strategic alignment. This isn’t a mere commercial transaction; it is a 30-year loyalty contract,” said Boyko Nikolov, the founder of Hardpoint, a specialised strategic intelligence platform.
When nations like Germany or Finland acquired Israeli air defence systems, they would be locked into Israel via software, intelligence sharing and continuous technical support, he told This Week In Asia.
This would transform Israel from a regional power into a “critical pillar” of European security, providing Israel with huge diplomatic leverage as it would be impossible to “isolate or sanction the state that holds the key to protecting your airspace”, Nikolov said.
In doing so, Israel has successfully leveraged its defence capabilities into its “most potent foreign policy tool” in which it is “a primary provider and guarantor for the West, fundamentally shifting its weight at the global negotiating table”.

Germany is the biggest buyer of air defence systems from Israel. Earlier this month, it began to deploy the Arrow 3 systems supplied by Israel Aerospace Industries (IAI) under a US$3.6 billion contract, with the first battery stationed at an airbase near Berlin. The two countries signed a separate US$3.1 billion deal for the supply of more Arrow 3 systems on December 18. Together, the deals were the largest-ever Israeli military export sale.
The Arrow 3 acquisitions came as Germany seeks to strengthen its defences against the perceived growing threat of Russian ballistic missile attacks amid the ongoing Ukraine war, while doubts about the long-term commitment of the US towards Nato continue to concern the alliance’s European partners.
Air-defence procurement creates long-term dependence, requiring continuous access to spare parts, software updates, training and upgrades, according to Andreas Krieg, an associate professor of defence studies at King’s College London.
“That produces weaponised interdependence,” Krieg said, as each customer’s operational readiness was tied to Israel’s supply chains and technical support.
Over time, this can include intelligence exchanges, joint exercises and interoperability arrangements, especially when customers want deeper integration of their air defence systems with those of allies, according to Krieg.
As an indication of Israel’s rising profile among European customers, Romania signed a US$2 billion-plus contract with Rafael Advanced Systems in July for the supply of six Spyder short-range units.
Greece is close to finalising a US$3 billion package for Rafael’s Spyder and medium-to-long range David’s Sling systems and IAI’s Barak MX integrated air and missile defence system to replace its existing Russian-made systems, according to IAI CEO Boaz Levy in a recent interview with the Israeli business news platform Globes.

Netanyahu leveraged this prospective deal at a summit with Greek Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis and Cypriot President Nikos Christodoulides in Jerusalem on Monday to issue a thinly veiled threat to Turkey, their common East Mediterranean rival.
Netanyahu said “those who fantasise they can reestablish their empires and their dominion over our lands” to “not even think about it”.
In a joint declaration, the three leaders pledged to strengthen their trilateral cooperation on security, defence and military matters.
Georgia received delivery of its Spyder systems this month.
Beyond Europe, Israel’s defence industry secured a US$108 million deal with Thailand on November 20 for the acquisition of Barak MX systems to protect its air force bases. Kenya is among Israel’s growing base of African customers, having recently taken delivery of several Spyder systems.
According to figures released by Israel’s defence ministry in July, air defence systems and associated missiles and rockets made up almost half of its arms exports in 2024, with Europe accounting for 54 per cent of the sales.
The reason for European countries’ preference for Israel’s air defence systems is due to the successful deployment of these weapons in combat situations in the Middle East and elsewhere, ranging from countering suicide drones to hypersonic ballistic missiles, according to Nikolov.
While major Western defence contractors were often bogged down in long development cycles and political bureaucracy, Israeli systems like Arrow-3 and David’s Sling had operated under constant and high-intensity missile threats, he said.
You choose the one who has already won the war in the sky
Boyko Nikolov, Hardpoint’s founder
“When your security is on the line, and a conflict is at your doorstep, you choose the one who has already won the war in the sky, not the one with the best PowerPoint presentation,” Nikolov said.
Israel’s ability to deliver at speed and deeply integrate various technologies was something that its Western defence competitors could not match currently, he added.
Krieg agreed that Israel’s defence industry had been responding to the demand for systems that matched the current threat environment, with its array of weapons developed and continuously refined following their deployment on the battlefront.
“For buyers trying to close gaps quickly, the attraction is not only perceived effectiveness but also delivery and integration,” Krieg said.
With governments prioritising protection against missiles and drones, Israel could present itself as an indispensable security provider, according to Krieg.
“States relying on Israeli systems for critical national defence have reasons to avoid ruptures that could affect sustainment, readiness or upgrade paths.”
On Wednesday, Netanyahu announced plans to invest US$110 billion to expand Israel’s defence industry.
“Our goal is to build an independent arms industry for the ‍state of Israel and reduce the dependency on any party, including allies,” he said.

data.title: Hong Kong probes mainland Chinese driver for using banned autopilot mode in the city
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have vowed to investigate a mainland Chinese driver under the southbound travel vehicle scheme and may suspend his eligibility after he posted a video of himself using adaptive cruise control illegally in the city on social media.
The Transport Department said on Friday that it would issue a warning to the driver while urging Guangdong motorists under the “Southbound Travel for Guangdong Vehicles (Entry into Urban Area)” scheme to use Octopus cards for car park access and payment, and to comply with local traffic laws.
A video showing one scheme driver switching to adaptive cruise control in Hong Kong circulated on popular online platforms RedNote and Facebook.
In the video, the male was driving a Xpeng P7 sedan, made by the Chinese electric vehicle (EV) manufacturer, on the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge. He took his hands off the steering wheel for a period while the car was said to be controlled by the Navigation Guided Pilot system, an autonomous driving feature.
In response to the Post, the department said that as driver assistance systems involved processing road information and traffic rules, any automated driving system not approved by the department must not be used on Hong Kong roads.
“As Hong Kong’s traffic rules differ from those on the mainland, using this system may result in driving violations; therefore, such systems should not be used on Hong Kong roads,” it said.
The department said it was “seriously looking” into the case and would issue a warning to the driver for activating automatic cruising in Hong Kong. It added that his permit might be suspended.
“Unless the driver provides a reply to the satisfaction of the Transport Department, the travel booking eligibility will be suspended,” it said.
The department added that a notice had been issued to all users registered under the scheme reminding them not to use unauthorised automatic driving systems.
“Drivers using any driver control assistance systems without the Transport Department’s approval may commit an offence under traffic regulations, which is liable on conviction to fines or imprisonment,” it said.
In the notice, the department reminded drivers under the southbound scheme not to activate their vehicles’ autonomous driving systems while driving on Hong Kong roads, due to differences in traffic signs, signals and road markings between Hong Kong and the mainland.

The scheme, which allows cars from the neighbouring province to enter the city directly via the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge, began on Tuesday and permits 100 drivers to travel to Hong Kong each day.
Another social media post showed that a driver under the scheme was fined HK$320 (US$41) for illegally parking on Pitt Street in Yau Ma Tei.
The driver, who posted on RedNote, said he was unable to find a legal parking space after driving around the area.
“I was so hungry that I parked my car in a place without any yellow lines or parking meters...It’s not that I did not have the money for the parking fee, I just did not find a parking spot,” he wrote.
Police said drivers would be issued with an electronic fixed penalty notice for any traffic contravention, adding that authorities had the contact details of all applicants under the southbound scheme.
It added that those fined should settle their penalties via e-payment, in person while in Hong Kong, or through relatives.
Separately, on Thursday, the Transport Department posted parking tips on its social media accounts for scheme drivers after some complained they did not possess Octopus cards and that payment systems at some car parks were located on the right-hand side of vehicles.
The department said most car parks in Hong Kong accept Octopus payment, with 151 private and government-run facilities also supporting mainland payment methods.
It told the Post that both public and private car park operators were “proactively enhancing” their facilities to provide greater convenience for Guangdong drivers, including contactless parking for left-hand-drive vehicles, acceptance of mainland payment systems and installation of Chinese national standard charging facilities.
The department said that, after liaising with operators of popular mainland navigation apps, updates had been made to Hong Kong maps to display car parks with these enhanced facilities, particularly those near tourist hotspots.
“Guangdong drivers are encouraged to use Octopus for accessing and payment at most car parks,” it added.
Currently, more than 60 charging poles are in operation, with additional ones to be installed in the future.
The department added that most charging poles in Hong Kong car parks used the European standard and that mainland vehicle owners should bring suitable charging adaptors.

data.title: Your horoscopes for January 2026, the month of the Earth Ox: work, money and health
data.description:
What do the stars and ancient Chinese wisdom have in store for each of the 12 zodiac animal signs this month?
Read on for detailed predictions covering career prospects, relationship dynamics, health advice and overall fortune for the 12 signs.
Use these predictions by fortune-teller Andrew Kwan as a guide to navigate challenges and maximise your opportunities in the coming weeks.
Rat (1936, 1948, 1960, 1972, 1984, 1996, 2008, 2020)

Things are going steady and according to plan for Rats this month. Career-wise, it is better to be more prudent when making decisions at work, balanced with a humble attitude. Those engaged in creative fields can use more opportunities to put their best work forward.
Single Rats will be getting some attention in the love department, but these will tend to fizzle out. Those who are attached might find their relationship is getting a bit stale; they should give their partners a bit of extra attention.
Do not forget to drink lots of water as Rats are prone to problems with their urinary system and insomnia.
Ox (1937, 1949, 1961, 1973, 1985, 1997, 2009, 2021)

Good fortunes befall Oxen this month. They may get a leg up from their boss to open up career opportunities, but Oxen should be careful of someone stabbing them in the back.
Single people may find romance in the workplace, while those with partners need to communicate more to avoid misunderstandings, which could lead to indifference in the relationship.
Oxen are recommended to pay attention to gastrointestinal health and avoid overindulgence.
Tiger (1938, 1950, 1962, 1974, 1986, 1998, 2010)

Work will be full of challenges and pressure for Tigers, however, those who are working abroad will have an opportunity to make a name for themselves.
Tigers might meet a special someone this month, but they will only be ships that pass in the night and not long term. Tigers with partners need to increase communication and support each other in this volatile month.
Work pressure will increase anxiety levels; it is advised to try to maintain a regular schedule and build resistance.
Rabbit (1939, 1951, 1963, 1975, 1987, 1999, 2011)

Work and finances are in good order for Rabbits, but networking and relationships will require their attention. Gossip, back-stabbing and miscommunication are rife this month and should be approached with patience and diplomacy.
Singles should avoid complications caused by casual relationships, while those with partners should be wary of too much attention from others, given to themselves as well as their spouses.
Rabbits should pay particular attention to rest, liver and gallbladder discomfort and injuries to hands and feet.
Dragon (1940, 1952, 1964, 1976, 1988, 2000, 2012)

This will be a stellar month for working Dragons, as many opportunities will arise. The people of this zodiac sign can try expanding into new markets or showcasing their creativity. However, they must be mindful of whether these creative ideas are feasible.
Single Dragons may meet good partners in academic settings or while volunteering, while those with partners might need to go out on a limb for their extended family.
Dragons should pay attention to diet, digestive problems and water retention.
Snake (1929, 1941, 1953, 1965, 1977, 1989, 2001, 2013)

Things at work are finally improving for Snakes, and challenges from previous months are going to be resolved. Those who travel a lot for work will find their fortune this month. However, Snakes are prone to additional expenses due to household matters such as maintenance.
Singletons may meet someone special while travelling, while those with partners are prone to disagreements over friendships or values.
Work stress is a bit high, and snakes should pay attention to cardiovascular and heart health. Walking outside in nature and paying attention to rest will be beneficial this month.
Horse (1930, 1942, 1954, 1966, 1978, 1990, 2002, 2014)

The situation is getting rocky at work for Horses. Conflicts are prominent, help from benefactors is limited, while bosses are flip-flopping on decisions, creating an extra burden for subordinates. Horses should look to their expenses as household appliances may break this month.
Horses with partners are more prone to conflict and need to focus on taking care of each other, while singles might engage in a passionate affair.
Eye discomfort and headaches are prevalent this month; adequate rest is recommended.
Sheep (1931, 1943, 1955, 1967, 1979, 1991, 2003, 2015)

Things will feel stagnant for Sheep at work this month. Plans will progress slowly, and there will be scheming back-stabbers and general difficulties at work. Work travel is not recommended.
Single Sheep should avoid workplace romances, which can be the source of malicious gossip, while those with a partner should focus on communicating more with each other.
Chances of minor illnesses are relatively high this month. Conditions like upset stomachs, skin irritations or recurrence of chronic conditions are likely, and professional help should be sought as soon as possible.
Monkey (1932, 1944, 1956, 1968, 1980, 1992, 2004, 2016)

Good fortune sits on Monkeys’ shoulders at work, so it is time to take on new tasks and showcase creativity. Working as a team produces results and increased revenue. However, there will also be added stress and strain.
Single Monkeys will be lucky in love, while those with a partner will deepen their connection through shared interests and activities.
People of this zodiac sign are prone to respiratory diseases. It is recommended to maintain good sleep and exercise habits.
Rooster (1933, 1945, 1957, 1969, 1981, 1993, 2005, 2017)

It will be smooth sailing at the workplace for Roosters this month. There will be chances to work to their strengths, which means excelling at existing projects. Challenges may arise or emergency situations, and it is advised to be prepared and flexible.
Single Roosters will be lucky in love, but will also tend to have disagreements with those around them. Roosters with children will argue with their significant other about how to educate or entertain them.
Attention should be paid to avoid respiratory disease and afflictions of the vocal cords. It is recommended to maintain good sleep and exercise habits to improve physical fitness and resistance.
Dog (1934, 1946, 1958, 1970, 1982, 1994, 2006, 2018)

The situation at work for Dogs has improved from last month, but things remain thorny and stressful, and feelings of isolation and fatigue prevail.
On top of that, it would be difficult to meet new partners this month or even meet up with people. Dogs with partners would benefit from being honest and direct to avoid miscommunication.
Pay attention to stress-induced digestive and sleep quality problems. It is advisable to have a regular schedule and exercise to help relax.
Pig (1935, 1947, 1959, 1971, 1983, 1995, 2007, 2019)

Competition at work is fierce for Pigs, and budgeting will be a bit tight this month. Seek help, and chances are, benefactors will come to your aid.
Singles may get into complicated relationships due to the influence of bad company, while those with a partner may get into money disputes or situations. It is advised to keep a level head.
Be aware of urinary or reproductive system problems, and it is also a good month to stay away from water sports.

data.title: Inside Chernobyl: Ukraine races to repair radiation shield as war drags on
data.description:
Inside an abandoned control room at Ukraine’s Chernobyl nuclear power plant, a worker in an orange hard hat gazed at a grey wall of seemingly endless dials, screens and gauges that were supposed to prevent disaster.
The 1986 meltdown at the site was the world’s worst ever nuclear incident. Since Russia invaded in 2022, Kyiv fears another disaster could be just a matter of time.
In February, a Russian drone hit and left a large hole in the New Safe Confinement (NSC), the outer of two radiation shells covering the remnants of the nuclear power plant.
It functions as a modern hi-tech replacement for an inner steel-and-concrete structure - known as the Sarcophagus, a defensive layer built hastily after the 1986 incident.

Ten months later, repair work is still ongoing, and it could take another three to four years before the outer dome regains its primary safety functions, plant director Sergiy Tarakanov said in an interview from Kyiv.
“It does not perform the function of retaining radioactive substances inside,” Tarakanov said, echoing concerns raised by the International Atomic Energy Agency.
The strike had also left it unclear if the shell would last the 100 years it was designed to.
The gaping crater in the structure, which Agence France-Presse journalists saw this summer, has been covered over with a protective screen, but 300 smaller holes made by firefighters when battling the blaze still need to be filled in.
Scaffolding engulfs the inside of the giant multibillion-dollar structure, rising all the way up to the 100 metre high ceiling.
Charred debris from the drone strike that hit the NSC still lay on the floor of the plant, Agence France-Presse journalists saw on a visit to the site in December.

Russia’s army captured the plant on the first day of its 2022 invasion, before withdrawing a few weeks later.
Ukraine has repeatedly accused Moscow of targeting Chernobyl and its other nuclear power plants, saying Moscow’s strikes risk triggering a potentially catastrophic disaster.
Ukraine regularly reduces power at its nuclear plants following Russian strikes on its energy grid.
In October, a Russian strike on a substation near Chernobyl cut power flowing to the confinement structure.
Tarakanov said that radiation levels at the site had remained “stable and within normal limits”.
Inside a modern control room, engineer Ivan Tykhonenko was keeping track of 19 sensors and detection units, constantly monitoring the state of the site.

Part of the 190 tonnes of uranium that were on site in 1986 “melted, sank down into the reactor unit, the sub-reactor room, and still exists,” he said.
Worries over the fate of the site - and what could happen - run high.
Another Russian hit - or even a powerful nearby strike - could see the inner radiation shell collapse, director Tarakanov said.
“If a missile or drone hits it directly, or even falls somewhere nearby … it will cause a mini-earthquake in the area,” he said.
“No one can guarantee that the shelter facility will remain standing after that. That is the main threat,” he added.
The escalating danger at Chernobyl has added a sense of urgency to the intensifying diplomatic efforts to end the conflict.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said on Thursday that he spoke ‌with US President Donald Trump’s special envoy Steve Witkoff and son-in-law ‍Jared Kushner for about an hour on how to end the war with Russia.
“It was a really good conversation: many details, good ideas, that we discussed. There are some new ⁠ideas on how to bring the real peace closer, and it concerns formats, meetings, and, certainly, the timeline,” Zelensky said on the Telegram app.
Trump has been pushing for a deal to end the almost four-year-long war, and in recent weeks peace ‍efforts led by Witkoff and Kushner have been slowly inching forward.
Earlier this week, ‍Zelensky presented a 20-point draft peace plan that he described as the main framework for ending the war.
The draft represented ‌a slimmed-down version of an original 28-point plan the US previously discussed with the Russian side ‍that was widely seen as mainly benefiting Moscow as it demanded Kyiv cede territory and put curbs on its army.
However, key territorial questions remain unresolved in the new 20-point draft, Zelensky said, adding ‌that ⁠a meeting with Trump would be required to solve the most sensitive issues.
Kremlin spokesperson Dmitry Peskov said on Thursday that Moscow was analysing the documents on ending the war which were brought to Moscow by Russia’s special envoy Kirill Dmitriev ‌from the United States.
Additional reporting by Reuters

data.title: China’s reusable rocket failure, PLA’s growth leaves US ‘vulnerable’: SCMP’s 7 highlights
data.description:
We have selected seven stories from this week’s news across Hong Kong, mainland China, the wider Asia region and beyond that resonated with our readers and shed light on topical issues. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. Pentagon says Chinese aircraft carriers, 6th-gen fighters make US ‘vulnerable’
A Pentagon report contends that China’s development of its aircraft carriers, sixth-generation fighters and nuclear arsenal illustrates Beijing’s “historic” growth in military power and has left the US “increasingly vulnerable”.
2. Second reusable rocket recovery failure in a month puts China 10 years behind US

The Long March 12A, China’s first state-owned reusable rocket, made its debut launch on Tuesday morning but the recovery of the first stage was a failure. It marked China’s second failed bid this month to return an orbital-class booster to Earth, a feat that has so far only been achieved by the United States.
3. China, Russia clash with US at UN over Venezuela oil seizures and naval pressure
China accused the United States at the United Nations on Tuesday of violating international law and destabilising the Caribbean region, as Washington defended its seizure of Venezuelan oil tankers and vowed to intensify pressure on President Nicolas Maduro’s government.
4. Malaysian court blocks Najib’s bid for house arrest

A Malaysian court on Monday dismissed former prime minister Najib Razak’s bid to serve the remainder of his prison sentence under house arrest, dealing a setback to his attempt to secure early release from jail.
5. Thousands stuck for hours at Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge on Christmas Day

Tens of thousands of people were stuck for hours at the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau Bridge early on Christmas Day, while the number of outbound trips by Hongkongers as of Thursday night saw an about 16 per cent rise from last year.
6. Singapore’s young graduates feel the heat in ‘draining’ job hunt
A dearth of entry-level jobs has forced some young Singaporeans to pivot to stopgap roles and entrepreneurship.
7. China boy breaks US$280,000 gold crown on display; owner will not pursue damages

An influencer in China has said online that a golden phoenix crown her artist husband made her as a wedding gift was damaged by a child and her mother while taking a photograph at an exhibition.

data.title: China vows to keep up the fight after record number of ‘tigers’ caught in corruption net
data.description:
China’s ruling Communist Party has vowed to keep up efforts to fight corruption next year after a record 63 high-ranking officials – known as “tigers” – were placed under investigation on suspicion of graft in 2025.
During a meeting on Thursday, top decision-making body the Politburo also discussed a plan to improve conduct within the party, strengthen integrity and combat graft, official news agency Xinhua reported.
It said officials at the meeting had agreed to “resolutely push forward the fight against corruption, not stopping for a moment, not yielding an inch, and [to] deepen the comprehensive approach to addressing both the symptoms and root causes [of corruption]”.
The Politburo also called for the party’s discipline inspection and supervision bodies to push ahead next year with “higher standards and more effective measures” to provide a strong “guarantee” for China’s economic and social development in the five years to 2030.
It said deepening and solidifying “political supervision” to maintain officials’ loyalty to the leadership would remain a priority for these bodies.


In addition, the Politburo called for the agencies to help establish a “correct view of performance” among party members and cadres and to ensure the leadership’s key decisions were effectively implemented in the next five years, according to the report.
President Xi Jinping had earlier presided over a meeting of the Politburo Standing Committee, whose seven members are the most senior in the party.
They heard a work report for 2025 from the top discipline and anti-corruption bodies, the Central Commission for Discipline Inspection and the National Supervisory Commission, Xinhua said.
A decision to hold the CCDI’s fifth plenary session from January 12 to 14 was endorsed – a meeting that will chart the graft-buster’s work priorities for 2026.
The pledge to continue long-running efforts to combat corruption in China came a day after the CCDI announced that Wang Jun, a former deputy disciplinary chief in Tibet, had been detained.
Wang, who was also deputy director of Tibet’s regional legislature, became the 63rd tiger to be placed under investigation for corruption this year.
That is a new record since Xi launched the sweeping anti-corruption campaign in 2013. This year’s figure is about 9 per cent higher than the 58 senior officials caught in the corruption net last year, which was also a record high.
These “tigers” are among the country’s so-called centrally managed cadres that are usually ranked at the deputy ministerial level or above. Some hold slightly lower ranks but occupy key positions in important sectors.
They are directly managed by the party’s Central Organisation Department, its top personnel body, and face top-level investigation by the CCDI if they are suspected of any wrongdoing.
Dozens of top Chinese generals have also been removed this year in an ongoing crackdown on corruption in the military.
Among the most high-profile dismissals were He Weidong, a former Central Military Commission vice-chairman and a member of the Politburo, and Miao Hua, who was the ideology and personnel chief of the People’s Liberation Army.


Separately, the CCDI on Wednesday released new guidelines for its investigators on obtaining and securing evidence.
A report on the CCDI website said the guidelines covered more than 20 types of corruption with a focus on “new and hidden forms” and they outlined key points for evidence collection and clarified evidentiary standards based on the elements of a crime.
The CCDI said it had sought feedback on the guidelines from legislative and judicial bodies.
In April, the graft-buster said corruption had become more sophisticated in recent years, with officials involved finding new ways to stay out of view.
That included waiting until after they had retired to receive bribes, and “revolving door” arrangements where officials used their knowledge for personal gain.

data.title: China’s C919 maker teams up with China Eastern with a bigger variant on the cards: sources
data.description:
China’s state-owned planemaker intends to supersize its flagship jet through a partnership with a major domestic carrier to meet rising market demand for bigger narrowbody types, according to sources.
The Commercial Aircraft Corp of China (Comac) signed an agreement with China Eastern Airlines last week to deepen cooperation, including on a stretched variant of the single-aisle C919 that would be able to seat at least 200 passengers, the Post has learned.
The deal positions China Eastern, the C919’s launch customer, to play a key role in the development, certification and trials of the new model, which is likely to be designated the C919-800, one of the sources said.
The current baseline version of the C919 can seat 158-192 people. Comac has not yet announced a timeline for the extended version’s certification or entry into service.
Jason Zheng, an analyst with Shanghai-based aviation consultancy Airwefly, said Comac’s move to team up with airline partners will enhance the C919’s appeal and marketability.
“China Eastern has over two years of experience flying the standard C919, and its feedback from day-to-day use will be valuable to optimise the design of the stretched model,” he said. “Once China Eastern has a say on how to enhance the design, the carrier may be more likely to place new orders.”
The standard C919 began commercial flights in May 2023 – a critical step towards chipping away at the long-standing global duopoly in large commercial aircraft held by Boeing and Airbus.


Comac plans to expand the family with an extended version for trunk routes and a shortened variant for high-altitude airports such as those in Tibet.
Zheng said that Comac, in the face of fierce competition, has to up the ante.
“In aircraft design and marketing, even in the category of narrowbodies, there is no one-size-fits-all solution,” he said. “When single-aisle jets with a higher seating capacity and more versatility, like the Airbus A320neo, are gaining popularity among global carriers, the C919 also needs to evolve.”
Similar to the subvariants offered by the competing Boeing 737 Max and Airbus A320neo families, the C919’s shortened and extended versions would have cabin classes spanning 130 to 240 seats, according to the preliminary design parameters released by Comac in 2023.
Aside from seating, range also looks to be a determining factor for the stretched C919, with Zheng saying it would likely be able to fly “more than 200 passengers for 4,500 kilometres or more, meaning it could operate international flights between China’s coastal cities and major hubs and tourism hotspots in most parts of Asia, especially in Southeast Asia”.
Our particular route structure and market demand require a larger narrowbody aircraft
Alex McGowan, Cathay Pacific
Brian Yang, a China-based industry consultant, said airlines increasingly need large narrowbodies to complement widebody fleets on medium-haul routes with fluctuating demand, giving carriers “the flexibility to deploy either a widebody or a big narrowbody in response to ridership changes”.
While Yang expects widebodies to continue dominating China’s trunk routes – such as those connecting Shanghai, Beijing, Guangzhou and Shenzhen – he said the C919-800 could serve international networks from wealthy second-tier cities to destinations across Southeast Asia.
Yang added that carriers may also favour the C919-800 for long routes between coastal cities and western parts of the country, such as the Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region.
The prospect of a larger jet has already piqued interest abroad.
Cathay Pacific’s chief operations and service delivery officer, Alex McGowan, said in March that Hong Kong’s flagship carrier would be interested in a stretched C919.
“I think our particular route structure and market demand require a larger narrowbody aircraft,” McGowan said at a news conference. “So, we’re interested to see if Comac’s plans … will include the introduction of a stretched variant of the C919.”
However, significant hurdles remain. Zheng said it could take years to finalise the design and obtain safety certification from Chinese regulators, pointing to challenges such as ensuring the integrity of the extended fuselage and proving emergency-evacuation capabilities in a high-density cabin.
